questions.section: Database and Cloud Security
questions.question: What is the main reason for discussing data first?

choices.text: Almost every modern application relies on data such as passwords, financial records, or personal messages.
Modern applications rarely store information because cloud providers keep everything temporary.
Only financial applications rely on data; messaging and passwords are not included.
Applications mainly need data centers, not databases or cloud infrastructure.
Data is important only after attackers have already stolen database files.

questions.explanation: The introduction says almost every modern application relies on data and gives passwords, financial records, and personal messages as examples.

questions.section: Database and Cloud Security
questions.question: After storing data, what must also be ensured?

choices.text: The data must be stored only in plain text files for simplicity.
The data must remain available, accurate over time, and protected from unauthorized access.
The data must be copied to every user device without access controls.
The data must be encrypted only after it is no longer needed.
The data must be visible to everyone so applications can scale quickly.

questions.explanation: Availability, accuracy over time, and protection from unauthorized access are requirements after storage.

questions.section: Database and Cloud Security
questions.question: What combination is required to protect modern data?

choices.text: Simple text files plus public cloud storage with no extra security.
RAID alone, because physical availability solves every security issue.
Strong database management plus secure cloud infrastructure.
Encryption alone, because it removes the need for access control.
A web application alone, because the database should trust all inputs.

questions.explanation: The introduction says strong database management and increasingly secure cloud infrastructure are required.

questions.section: Database and Cloud Security
questions.question: Which set best matches the scope of this topic?

choices.text: Only physical server cooling and raised floor design.
Only how to buy hardware firewalls for a local office.
Only how to write SQL queries faster for reporting.
Database structure, protection from internal mistakes and external attacks, and cloud security changes.
Only how to compare Gmail and Salesforce pricing.

questions.explanation: It explores database structure, protection from internal and external threats, and how security changes in the cloud.

questions.section: How Data is Stored and Managed
questions.question: Why do applications not simply write important data into a text file?

choices.text: Text files automatically provide RAID, authorization tables, and parameterized queries.
Text files are always more secure than database management systems.
Applications do not need to remember information after users close them.
Cloud providers forbid all databases and require text files instead.
They need a dedicated system to organize, find, and protect data efficiently.

questions.explanation: The section explains that applications need a dedicated system rather than simple text files.

questions.section: How Data is Stored and Managed
questions.question: What fundamental physical problem must be solved before complex database security rules?

choices.text: Hard drives can break, and if the drive holding the database fails, the data is lost.
Users can guess weak passwords before data is ever stored.
SQL Injection can only happen after a disk is mirrored.
Cloud APIs can delete infrastructure before a database exists.
Views can reveal all columns unless they are encrypted.

questions.explanation: Hard drive failure is the basic physical availability problem.

questions.section: How Data is Stored and Managed
questions.question: What does RAID stand for?

choices.text: Reliable Access for Internal Databases.
Redundant Array of Independent Disks.
Remote Array of Indexed Data.
Role Authorization and Identity Directory.
Runtime Analysis of Injection Defenses.

questions.explanation: The source expands RAID as Redundant Array of Independent Disks.

questions.section: How Data is Stored and Managed
questions.question: What is the basic idea behind RAID?

choices.text: One drive is encrypted so no other storage devices are required.
A DBMS replaces physical hard drives with browser cookies.
Multiple drives are connected together so they act as one large unit.
Every user receives a separate physical hard drive.
SQL privileges are copied across roles automatically.

questions.explanation: RAID connects multiple drives so they function together as one large storage unit.

questions.section: How Data is Stored and Managed
questions.question: What does RAID mirroring provide?

choices.text: It splits data across drives mainly to make reading and writing faster.
It encrypts sensitive columns such as passwords and credit card numbers.
It assigns permissions to users through an authorization table.
It copies data across drives so the same information still exists if one drive breaks.
It forces stolen data to be sent to an attacker-controlled server.

questions.explanation: The source says mirroring copies data so another drive still has the same information after one drive fails.

questions.section: How Data is Stored and Managed
questions.question: What does RAID striping do?

choices.text: It copies the same data onto multiple drives for failure survival only.
It hides sensitive table columns from unauthorized users.
It checks whether a user has permission to modify a record.
It routes office network traffic through a cloud security provider.
It splits data across drives to make reading and writing much faster.

questions.explanation: Striping splits data across drives for faster read and write operations.

questions.section: How Data is Stored and Managed
questions.question: What availability outcome does RAID provide?

choices.text: A single hardware failure should not bring down the entire system.
A single SQL Injection attempt should automatically encrypt all records.
A single user role should be able to access every database object.
A single cloud API key should control all provider customers.
A single view should expose all sensitive columns for convenience.

questions.explanation: RAID provides fundamental availability by preventing one disk failure from taking down the whole system.

questions.section: How Data is Stored and Managed
questions.question: What does DBMS stand for?

choices.text: Database Mirroring Service.
Database Management System.
Distributed Backup Management Server.
Data Breach Mitigation Standard.
Dynamic Browser Message Store.

questions.explanation: DBMS is expanded as Database Management System.

questions.section: How Data is Stored and Managed
questions.question: Where does the DBMS sit?

choices.text: Between the attacker and their out-of-band server.
Between the cloud customer and the provider billing page only.
Between a user role and a human resources job title.
Between the application and the physical data.
Between a public cloud and a private cloud in hybrid deployment only.

questions.explanation: The source defines the DBMS as the software layer between applications and physical data.

questions.section: How Data is Stored and Managed
questions.question: What standardized operations does the DBMS provide?

choices.text: Phishing administrator accounts and deleting infrastructure.
Creating cloud firewalls and scanning files for malware only.
Encrypting entire databases without needing a key.
Splitting hot and cold data across public websites.
Creating, modifying, and retrieving information safely.

questions.explanation: The DBMS provides a standardized way to create, modify, and retrieve information safely.

questions.section: How Data is Stored and Managed
questions.question: What does concurrent access management handle?

choices.text: Multiple cloud customers paying for exactly what they consume.
Multiple users or processes trying to read or change the same shared data simultaneously.
Multiple drives acting as one large storage unit only.
Multiple service models deciding who can rent an application.
Multiple firewalls replacing parameterized queries.

questions.explanation: Concurrent access concerns simultaneous reads or changes against shared data.

questions.section: How Data is Stored and Managed
questions.question: How can a DBMS keep data accurate during concurrent access?

choices.text: By letting the fastest request overwrite all slower requests.
By trusting cookies and server variables without validation.
By carefully locking records or placing transactions in order.
By moving all users to a public cloud deployment model.
By encrypting the RAID controller instead of managing transactions.

questions.explanation: The DBMS maintains integrity by locking records or ordering transactions.

questions.section: How Data is Stored and Managed
questions.question: How do relational databases organize data?

choices.text: Into browser cookies that are trusted by the application.
Into one unstructured text file per user.
Into security dashboards operated by the cloud provider.
Into structured tables, much like spreadsheets.
Into physical sensors and scanners only.

questions.explanation: Relational databases organize information into structured tables like spreadsheets.

questions.section: How Data is Stored and Managed
questions.question: What is a primary key?

choices.text: A link from one table to another table.
A saved search result that behaves like a virtual table.
A permission to read database data.
A malicious SQL string stored for later execution.
A unique identifier for every row in a table.

questions.explanation: A primary key uniquely identifies each row.

questions.section: How Data is Stored and Managed
questions.question: Why is a Student ID a good primary key?

choices.text: No two students have the same ID.
It links an Order table to a Customer table.
It hides sensitive columns through a saved search.
It grants update privileges to every student.
It stores encrypted passwords and credit card numbers.

questions.explanation: Student ID is an example because it is unique for each student.

questions.section: How Data is Stored and Managed
questions.question: What is a foreign key?

choices.text: A unique identifier for every row in one table.
A way to link two tables together.
A database view that hides sensitive columns.
A role that groups job-based permissions.
A cloud service model where users rent raw hardware.

questions.explanation: A foreign key connects related rows across tables.

questions.section: How Data is Stored and Managed
questions.question: How might an Order table use a foreign key?

choices.text: It might contain a Student ID to guarantee no two students match.
It might contain a view that reveals only authorized columns.
It might contain a Customer ID to show which customer made the purchase.
It might contain a cloud provider API key for infrastructure deletion.
It might contain a RAID stripe for faster disk writes.

questions.explanation: The source uses Customer ID in an Order table as the foreign key example.

questions.section: How Data is Stored and Managed
questions.question: What is a database view?

choices.text: A physical copy of every disk in a RAID array.
A password guessing attack against a cloud administrator.
A SQL command that takes privileges away from a user.
A custom, saved search result that acts like a new virtual table.
A cloud deployment dedicated to one organization.

questions.explanation: A view is defined as a saved result that behaves like a virtual table.

questions.section: How Data is Stored and Managed
questions.question: How can a view improve database security?

choices.text: It can make hard drives impossible to break.
It can execute user input as SQL code safely.
It can replace every need for cloud access controls.
It can give all users update permission by default.
It can show only permitted columns while hiding sensitive information.

questions.explanation: Views are often used to limit users to specific columns they are allowed to see.

questions.section: Protecting the Database from Inside: Access Control
questions.question: After data is stored and structured, what is the next access-control problem?

choices.text: Controlling who is allowed to see or change the data.
Choosing whether hard drives should stripe or mirror blocks.
Deciding whether a cloud provider has massive data centers.
Making all data public so applications can retrieve it faster.
Replacing every SQL command with browser cookies.

questions.explanation: The access control section starts with who can see or change data.

questions.section: Protecting the Database from Inside: Access Control
questions.question: Which security goal is explicitly tied to strict access rules?

choices.text: Elasticity.
Confidentiality.
Measured service.
Striping.
Out-of-band delivery.

questions.explanation: Strict rules are needed to protect confidentiality.

questions.section: Protecting the Database from Inside: Access Control
questions.question: What is the purpose of an authorization table?

choices.text: It physically mirrors data across drives to survive disk failure.
It stores malicious SQL strings until an administrator views them.
It lets the database check whether a user or role has the required permission for a specific action.
It routes office network traffic through a cloud security provider.
It measures how much cloud service a customer consumed.

questions.explanation: The authorization table enforces access rules by checking permissions for users or roles.

questions.section: Protecting the Database from Inside: Access Control
questions.question: What happens if a user is not authorized for a specific database action?

choices.text: The database automatically creates a new role for the user.
The request is converted into an encrypted column.
The DBMS treats the action as harmless application data.
The cloud provider mirrors the data across customers.
Access is denied.

questions.explanation: If the user or role is not on the authorization list for that action, the database denies access.

questions.section: Protecting the Database from Inside: Access Control
questions.question: Which administrative policy has one administrator or security team control all permissions?

choices.text: Centralized.
Ownership-based.
Decentralized.
Role-based only.
Multi-tenant.

questions.explanation: Centralized permission management uses one administrator or dedicated security team.

questions.section: Protecting the Database from Inside: Access Control
questions.question: Which administrative policy lets the data creator decide who else may see or change it?

choices.text: Centralized.
Ownership-based.
Decentralized.
Measured service.
Public cloud.

questions.explanation: Ownership-based policies give permission decisions to the person who created the data.

questions.section: Protecting the Database from Inside: Access Control
questions.question: Which administrative policy lets different departments or teams manage their own access controls independently?

choices.text: Centralized.
Ownership-based.
Decentralized.
Parameterized.
Inband.

questions.explanation: Decentralized access control lets teams or departments manage permissions independently.

questions.section: Protecting the Database from Inside: Access Control
questions.question: What does the SQL Grant command do?

choices.text: It takes a privilege away from a user.
It reads data from a table.
It creates a foreign key to this data.
It gives a privilege to a user.
It builds safe query objects instead of strings.

questions.explanation: Grant is listed as the command that gives a privilege.

questions.section: Protecting the Database from Inside: Access Control
questions.question: What does the SQL Revoke command do?

choices.text: It gives a privilege to a user.
It adds new data to a table.
It deletes the entire database after injection.
It creates a finished cloud application.
It takes a privilege away from a user.

questions.explanation: Revoke is listed as the command that removes a privilege.

questions.section: Protecting the Database from Inside: Access Control
questions.question: What does the Select privilege allow?

choices.text: Reading data.
Adding new data.
Removing data.
Modifying existing data.
Linking tables with a foreign key.

questions.explanation: Select is the permission to read data.

questions.section: Protecting the Database from Inside: Access Control
questions.question: What does the Insert privilege allow?

choices.text: Reading data.
Adding new data.
Removing data.
Modifying existing data.
Creating a foreign key link.

questions.explanation: Insert is the permission to add new data.

questions.section: Protecting the Database from Inside: Access Control
questions.question: What does the Delete privilege allow?

choices.text: Reading data.
Adding new data.
Removing data.
Modifying existing data.
Linking a table to this data.

questions.explanation: Delete is the permission to remove data.

questions.section: Protecting the Database from Inside: Access Control
questions.question: What does the Update privilege allow?

choices.text: Reading data only.
Adding brand-new data only.
Removing data only.
Modifying existing data.
Creating a foreign key only.

questions.explanation: Update is the permission to modify existing data.

questions.section: Protecting the Database from Inside: Access Control
questions.question: What does the References privilege allow?

choices.text: Reading data without seeing sensitive columns.
Adding new rows to the protected table.
Deleting rows from the protected table.
Changing all existing values in the protected table.
Linking a user's own tables to this data by creating a foreign key.

questions.explanation: References permits linking tables through a foreign key.

questions.section: Protecting the Database from Inside: Access Control
questions.question: What does RBAC stand for?

choices.text: Role-Based Access Control.
Redundant Backup Access Controller.
Runtime Breach Alert Console.
Relational Block Authorization Code.
Resource-Based Application Cloud.

questions.explanation: RBAC is expanded as Role-Based Access Control.

questions.section: Protecting the Database from Inside: Access Control
questions.question: What administrative problem does the 500 customer support agents example illustrate?

choices.text: Support agents cannot use relational databases at all.
Assigning permissions individually would be slow and prone to mistakes.
A DBMS cannot handle more than 500 concurrent users.
Every support agent needs their own physical cloud database.
SQL Injection is caused by too many support agents.

questions.explanation: The example of 500 customer support agents shows why individual permission assignment does not scale well.

questions.section: Protecting the Database from Inside: Access Control
questions.question: What does RBAC shift the focus toward?

choices.text: Physical disks instead of permissions.
Cloud providers instead of customers.
Job functions instead of individuals.
Encryption keys instead of database roles.
Browser cookies instead of login forms.

questions.explanation: RBAC focuses permission assignment on roles or job functions.

questions.section: Protecting the Database from Inside: Access Control
questions.question: In the RBAC example, what role is created?

choices.text: Database Owner.
Cloud Provider.
Security Guard.
Support Agent.
Primary Key.

questions.explanation: The administrator creates a role called Support Agent in the example.

questions.section: Protecting the Database from Inside: Access Control
questions.question: How does RBAC simplify changes when someone changes jobs or leaves?

choices.text: All permissions must be manually removed from every table one by one.
Their old account is kept active so queries do not break.
The database is encrypted and the key is discarded.
Their data is moved from public cloud to community cloud.
Access is updated by changing their role.

questions.explanation: RBAC makes access easier to update because the user-role assignment can be changed.

questions.section: Protecting the Database from Outside: SQL Injection (SQLi)
questions.question: Why can SQL Injection matter even when internal access controls are well configured?

choices.text: An insecure web application connected to the database can let an external attacker bypass the rules.
RAID mirroring disables authorization tables by design.
Cloud elasticity automatically grants attackers database roles.
Encryption keys are always stored in login boxes.
Views must execute every search term as SQL code.

questions.explanation: The SQLi section warns that a vulnerable web application can bypass otherwise good internal controls.

questions.section: Protecting the Database from Outside: SQL Injection (SQLi)
questions.question: What is SQL Injection?

choices.text: An administrator grants a user permission to read data through SQL.
An attacker types malicious SQL commands into a normal input field, and an unchecked application executes them in the database.
A DBMS locks records to stop two users from buying the same seat.
A provider pools resources to serve many cloud customers invisibly.
A database encrypts columns so they are unreadable without a key.

questions.explanation: SQLi happens when malicious input is inserted into a SQL query and executed by the database.

questions.section: Protecting the Database from Outside: SQL Injection (SQLi)
questions.question: What normal application behavior creates an opportunity for SQL Injection?

choices.text: A website mirrors hard drives so data remains available after failure.
A website rents virtual servers from Amazon, Microsoft, or Google.
A website takes user text and inserts it into a database query to find results.
A website assigns Alice and Bob to a Support Agent role.
A website displays only allowed columns through a view.

questions.explanation: SQLi occurs when website input is used in a database query.

questions.section: Protecting the Database from Outside: SQL Injection (SQLi)
questions.question: Which normal input fields are given as examples for SQL Injection?

choices.text: A RAID controller or disk stripe.
A cloud firewall or IAM console.
A private cloud or community cloud.
A login box or search bar.
A primary key or foreign key constraint.

questions.explanation: Login boxes and search bars are normal fields attackers may abuse.

questions.section: Protecting the Database from Outside: SQL Injection (SQLi)
questions.question: Which set of impacts can SQL Injection have?

choices.text: Only slow down RAID striping, with no security impact.
Only create a database view, without changing real data.
Only improve elasticity by adding cloud servers.
Only revoke privileges from the attacker automatically.
Steal sensitive data, modify records, or delete the entire database.

questions.explanation: The source says SQLi can steal data, modify records, or delete the entire database.

questions.section: Protecting the Database from Outside: SQL Injection (SQLi)
questions.question: Which security properties does SQL Injection threaten at once?

choices.text: Confidentiality, integrity, and availability.
Elasticity, measured service, and resource pooling.
Select, insert, and references only.
Centralized, ownership-based, and decentralized policies.
Public, private, and hybrid deployment models.

questions.explanation: SQLi threatens confidentiality, integrity, and availability all at once.

questions.section: Protecting the Database from Outside: SQL Injection (SQLi)
questions.question: What is an inband SQL Injection attack?

choices.text: The attacker infers results from subtle page changes or response delays.
The attacker sends the malicious query and sees stolen data immediately in the normal application interface.
The attacker forces the database to send data to a different server they control.
The attacker stores a malicious username that triggers only when viewed later.
The attacker uses scanners or sensors to send data into the application.

questions.explanation: Inband is the direct technique where results appear in the normal interface.

questions.section: Protecting the Database from Outside: SQL Injection (SQLi)
questions.question: What is an inferential or blind SQL Injection attack?

choices.text: The attacker sees stolen data immediately in the normal interface.
The attacker forces the database to send stolen data to another server.
The attacker guesses what is happening by observing clues such as page differences or response delays.
The attacker receives a role called Support Agent.
The attacker gets data by reading only permitted view columns.

questions.explanation: Blind SQLi relies on indirect clues because the database output is not shown directly.

questions.section: Protecting the Database from Outside: SQL Injection (SQLi)
questions.question: What is an out-of-band SQL Injection attack?

choices.text: The attacker sees the stolen data immediately in the normal application interface.
The attacker infers data only from page load timing.
The attacker changes their role through a centralized administrator.
The attacker forces the database to send stolen data to a different server controlled by the attacker.
The attacker encrypts the database but loses the decryption key.

questions.explanation: Out-of-band SQLi exfiltrates data through a separate attacker-controlled server.

questions.section: Protecting the Database from Outside: SQL Injection (SQLi)
questions.question: Which SQL Injection avenue includes forms, search boxes, and login fields?

choices.text: Server Variables.
Cookies.
Second-Order Injection.
Physical Devices.
User Input.

questions.explanation: Forms, search boxes, and login fields are listed under User Input.

questions.section: Protecting the Database from Outside: SQL Injection (SQLi)
questions.question: What are server variables in the SQLi attack avenues list?

choices.text: Hidden information sent by the browser, such as network headers.
Small browser files used to remember the user.
Forms, search boxes, and login fields.
Scanners, sensors, or external systems sending data.
Malicious code stored in the database that triggers later.

questions.explanation: Server variables are described as hidden browser-sent information like network headers.

questions.section: Protecting the Database from Outside: SQL Injection (SQLi)
questions.question: How can cookies become an SQL Injection avenue?

choices.text: Cookies always prevent SQL Injection because they are stored in the browser.
If the application trusts a modified browser cookie, it might build a dangerous query.
Cookies are physical devices that send sensor data to the application.
Cookies are SQL commands that revoke privileges from a user.
Cookies force the database to send output to another server.

questions.explanation: Trusting modified cookies can lead to dangerous queries.

questions.section: Protecting the Database from Outside: SQL Injection (SQLi)
questions.question: What makes second-order injection different?

choices.text: The attacker immediately sees results in the normal interface.
The database sends stolen data to a different server immediately.
Malicious code is stored in the database first and triggers later when that record is used, such as when an administrator views it.
The attack uses only network headers and never stores data.
The attack is prevented only by RAID mirroring.

questions.explanation: Second-order injection is delayed: malicious input is stored first and executed later in another context.

questions.section: Protecting the Database from Outside: SQL Injection (SQLi)
questions.question: Which examples are listed under physical devices as SQL Injection avenues?

choices.text: Forms, search boxes, and login fields.
Network headers hidden in browser requests.
Small files stored in the browser to remember users.
Scanners, sensors, or external systems that send data into the application.
Parameterized placeholders inside SQL commands.

questions.explanation: Scanners, sensors, and external systems are data sources that can be manipulated.

questions.section: Protecting the Database from Outside: SQL Injection (SQLi)
questions.question: What security mindset is recommended for stopping SQL Injection?

choices.text: Trust all outside data if it comes from a logged-in browser.
Trust cookies because they are stored on the user device.
Trust server variables because users cannot see them directly.
Trust input only when RAID is enabled.
Treat all outside data as potentially dangerous.

questions.explanation: The countermeasures section says developers must treat all outside data as potentially dangerous.

questions.section: Protecting the Database from Outside: SQL Injection (SQLi)
questions.question: What do validation and sanitization do?

choices.text: They check input for expected characters and strip out dangerous symbols.
They write data to both cloud sites at the same time.
They encrypt the whole database without key management.
They create a role and assign all users to it.
They pool physical resources across many customers.

questions.explanation: Validation and sanitization check and clean user input before it reaches queries.

questions.section: Protecting the Database from Outside: SQL Injection (SQLi)
questions.question: Why are parameterized queries described as the most effective SQL Injection defense?

choices.text: They let users paste SQL directly into commands if they have a role.
They use safe placeholders so the database treats input as data, never as executable code.
They encrypt all input and discard the decryption key.
They detect anomalies by scanning web traffic signatures only.
They convert public clouds into private clouds for sensitive data.

questions.explanation: Parameterized queries prevent input from being interpreted as SQL code.

questions.section: Protecting the Database from Outside: SQL Injection (SQLi)
questions.question: What is the SQL DOM countermeasure described as?

choices.text: A database view that hides sensitive columns from users.
A cloud service that rents raw hardware and networks.
Building queries with safe programming objects instead of manually gluing text strings together.
A RAID method that splits data for faster disk writes.
A privilege that lets tables create foreign key links.

questions.explanation: SQL DOM is described as using safe programming objects rather than manual string concatenation.

questions.section: Protecting the Database from Outside: SQL Injection (SQLi)
questions.question: What does signature-based detection look for?

choices.text: Only unknown behavior with no pattern matching.
Only cloud billing records like a water bill.
Only physical hard drive failures in RAID arrays.
Known attack patterns in web traffic.
Only employee job function changes.

questions.explanation: The detection section says systems can look for known attack patterns called signatures.

questions.section: Protecting the Database from Outside: SQL Injection (SQLi)
questions.question: What does anomaly detection flag?

choices.text: Only known exact attack strings.
Only successful role assignments.
Only encrypted columns with missing keys.
Only resource pooling across customers.
Unusual database behavior.

questions.explanation: Anomaly detection flags unusual database behavior.

questions.section: Protecting the Database from Outside: SQL Injection (SQLi)
questions.question: What should developers do before publishing code?

choices.text: Scan their code for weaknesses.
Disable all access controls to simplify testing.
Store malicious input for administrators to view later.
Trust cookies so fewer validations are needed.
Move every database to public cloud automatically.

questions.explanation: Developers should scan their code for weaknesses before publishing it.

questions.section: Protecting the Database from Outside: SQL Injection (SQLi)
questions.question: What is run-time SQL Injection prevention?

choices.text: A DBMS locks records so two buyers cannot get the same seat.
Specialized firewalls monitor and block suspicious SQL queries while the application is running.
A cloud provider pools resources for multiple customers invisibly.
A private cloud dedicates infrastructure to one organization.
A view shows only permitted columns from a saved query.

questions.explanation: Run-time prevention uses specialized firewalls to block suspicious SQL queries during operation.

questions.section: The Last Line of Defense: Database Encryption
questions.question: When is database encryption described as the last line of defense?

choices.text: When a developer needs to create a new server from a cloud dashboard.
When a user needs permission to add a row to a table.
When an attacker bypasses the web application, bypasses access controls, and steals the actual database files.
When two customers share the same multi-tenant database system.
When a RAID stripe speeds up reading and writing.

questions.explanation: The encryption section frames encryption as protection after multiple other defenses fail and files are stolen.

questions.section: The Last Line of Defense: Database Encryption
questions.question: What does encryption do to data?

choices.text: It grants a privilege to a user through SQL.
It splits data across drives for faster reads.
It lets many tenants share the same database safely by default.
It scrambles data so it is unreadable without a specific mathematical key.
It detects known attack patterns in web traffic.

questions.explanation: Encryption makes data unreadable unless the correct key is available.

questions.section: The Last Line of Defense: Database Encryption
questions.question: Which encryption scope options are listed?

choices.text: Only browser cookies, server variables, and login boxes.
Only public cloud, private cloud, and hybrid cloud.
Only Select, Insert, Delete, Update, and References.
Only inband, blind, and out-of-band attacks.
The entire database, specific tables, or sensitive columns.

questions.explanation: Encryption can cover the whole database, certain tables, or selected columns.

questions.section: The Last Line of Defense: Database Encryption
questions.question: Which sensitive columns are given as examples for encryption?

choices.text: Passwords and credit card numbers.
Customer IDs and order IDs only.
Network headers and cookies only.
Virtual servers and storage networks.
Support Agent roles and access policies.

questions.explanation: Passwords and credit card numbers are examples of highly sensitive columns.

questions.section: The Last Line of Defense: Database Encryption
questions.question: What happens if the decryption key is lost?

choices.text: The database automatically creates a replacement key.
The data is lost forever.
The data becomes public but remains readable.
The DBMS turns the encrypted column into a view.
The cloud provider converts the service to SaaS.

questions.explanation: The key management trade-off states that if you lose the decryption key, you lose the data forever.

questions.section: The Last Line of Defense: Database Encryption
questions.question: Why is encryption useless if an attacker steals both the database and the key?

choices.text: The attacker can only perform RAID striping, not read data.
The key automatically revokes the attacker privileges.
The attacker can decrypt the data because they have the required key.
The database becomes unavailable but still confidential.
The cloud provider cannot inspect physical servers anymore.

questions.explanation: Encryption depends on key secrecy; stealing both data and key defeats the protection.

questions.section: The Last Line of Defense: Database Encryption
questions.question: What is difficult about encryption keys?

choices.text: Using them to make RAID reads faster.
Using them to assign job functions to users.
Using them to pool cloud resources invisibly.
Safely storing and managing them.
Using them to create a saved virtual table.

questions.explanation: The key management bullet says safe storage and management are difficult.

questions.section: The Last Line of Defense: Database Encryption
questions.question: Why can encryption hurt database performance?

choices.text: Encrypted data always deletes itself after one query.
Encryption makes hard drives physically break more often.
Encryption prevents any user from being assigned a role.
Encryption forces every service to become public cloud.
Searching through scrambled text is very slow.

questions.explanation: The performance trade-off says searching scrambled text is slow.

questions.section: The Last Line of Defense: Database Encryption
questions.question: Besides search speed, what processing tasks become harder when data is encrypted?

choices.text: Filtering, sorting, and processing information efficiently.
Granting and revoking privileges only.
Creating raw virtual servers and networks only.
Sending stolen data to an out-of-band server only.
Pooling resources for many cloud customers only.

questions.explanation: Encrypted data is harder to filter, sort, and process efficiently.

questions.section: The Last Line of Defense: Database Encryption
questions.question: Why do organizations usually encrypt only their most sensitive data?

choices.text: Encryption works only on public data and cannot protect sensitive data.
Encryption has trade-offs, so firewalls and access controls often protect the rest.
Encryption eliminates the need for every other security control.
Encrypted columns cannot be stored in relational databases.
Cloud providers prohibit encryption of database tables.

questions.explanation: Because encryption has key and performance challenges, organizations reserve it for highly sensitive data.

questions.section: Moving Beyond the Local Server: Cloud Security
questions.question: What traditional database hosting approach is contrasted with cloud computing?

choices.text: Organizations always rented Gmail and Salesforce for every database workload.
Organizations stored databases only in browser cookies.
Organizations bought physical servers, placed them in a secure room, and ran databases there.
Organizations avoided DBMS software and used simple text files.
Organizations used only shared apartment multi-tenant databases.

questions.explanation: The cloud section contrasts cloud with buying and securing local physical servers.

questions.section: Moving Beyond the Local Server: Cloud Security
questions.question: What is cloud computing?

choices.text: Buying all hardware and placing it in a local secure room.
Encrypting specific database columns such as passwords.
Granting database privileges to users through SQL commands.
Renting computing resources such as servers, storage, and databases over the internet from a provider.
Saving user input directly into SQL commands.

questions.explanation: Cloud computing is defined as renting compute resources over the internet.

questions.section: Moving Beyond the Local Server: Cloud Security
questions.question: Which providers are named as cloud examples?

choices.text: Gmail, Salesforce, and Support Agent.
RAID, DBMS, and RBAC.
Select, Insert, and Update.
Inband, Blind, and Out-of-band.
Amazon, Microsoft, or Google.

questions.explanation: The source gives Amazon, Microsoft, and Google as provider examples.

questions.section: Moving Beyond the Local Server: Cloud Security
questions.question: Which benefits are listed for cloud computing?

choices.text: Innovate faster, scale resources instantly, and save money by only paying for what they use.
Avoid all access control, trust all input, and remove encryption keys.
Guarantee no cloud risks, no shared hardware, and no account hijacking.
Make every database a text file, remove DBMS software, and skip RAID.
Disable APIs, delete infrastructure, and avoid network access.

questions.explanation: Benefits include faster innovation, instant scaling, and saving money by only paying for usage.

questions.section: Moving Beyond the Local Server: Cloud Security
questions.question: What is resource pooling?

choices.text: A developer creates a server in seconds without speaking to a provider employee.
The provider pools massive data center resources to serve many customers simultaneously, invisibly to users.
The cloud automatically adds and removes servers as traffic changes.
Customers pay only for exactly what they consume.
Services are available over the internet from many device types.

questions.explanation: Resource pooling is provider-side sharing of large resource pools across many customers.

questions.section: Moving Beyond the Local Server: Cloud Security
questions.question: What is on-demand self-service?

choices.text: The provider pools massive resources to serve many customers invisibly.
The cloud adds servers when a website goes viral and removes them later.
A developer can create a new server or database from a dashboard in seconds without speaking to a human at the provider.
The customer pays only for what they consume like a utility bill.
Services are reachable from laptops, phones, or other servers anywhere.

questions.explanation: On-demand self-service means users provision resources themselves quickly through a dashboard.

questions.section: Moving Beyond the Local Server: Cloud Security
questions.question: What does elasticity mean when a website suddenly goes viral?

choices.text: The provider serves many customers from pooled resources invisibly.
A developer can create a database without talking to a human.
The customer pays exactly for consumed resources.
The cloud can automatically add servers for sudden traffic and remove them when traffic drops.
The service can be accessed from phones and laptops worldwide.

questions.explanation: Elasticity means that if a website suddenly goes viral, the cloud can add capacity and later remove it when demand drops.

questions.section: Moving Beyond the Local Server: Cloud Security
questions.question: What is measured service compared to?

choices.text: A guest list checked by a security guard.
A private house rented instead of an apartment.
A librarian finding a book in a library.
A login field accepting malicious SQL.
A water or electricity bill where you pay only for what you consume.

questions.explanation: Measured service is compared to utility billing based on exact consumption.

questions.section: Moving Beyond the Local Server: Cloud Security
questions.question: What does broad network access mean?

choices.text: Cloud services are available over the internet from laptops, phones, or other servers anywhere in the world.
Cloud services are available only from the provider physical data center.
Cloud services are available only to one department inside one office.
Cloud services are reachable only through local hard drives.
Cloud services require a provider employee to manually create each request.

questions.explanation: Broad network access means internet availability across device types and locations.

questions.section: Moving Beyond the Local Server: Cloud Security
questions.question: What do you rent in Infrastructure as a Service (IaaS)?

choices.text: A ready environment where you only deploy your code and database.
Raw hardware such as virtual servers, storage, and networks.
A finished application like Gmail or Salesforce.
A database view that hides sensitive columns.
A security application delivered as SaaS.

questions.explanation: IaaS rents raw infrastructure resources.

questions.section: Moving Beyond the Local Server: Cloud Security
questions.question: Which responsibility and trade-off belongs to IaaS?

choices.text: The provider handles servers and the operating system while you only deploy code.
You only log in and use a finished complete application.
You install your own operating system and software; it takes the most effort but gives the most control.
A provider delivers security applications over the internet as SaaS.
A provider dedicates infrastructure exclusively to one organization.

questions.explanation: IaaS gives maximum control but requires the customer to manage OS and software.

questions.section: Moving Beyond the Local Server: Cloud Security
questions.question: What is Platform as a Service (PaaS)?

choices.text: Raw hardware where you install your own operating system and software.
A finished complete application that you simply log in and use.
Infrastructure dedicated exclusively to one organization.
A ready environment for your code where the provider handles servers and the operating system.
A shared database where tenants are separated by identifiers.

questions.explanation: PaaS is an environment ready for your code: the provider handles the servers and operating system, while you deploy the application and database.

questions.section: Moving Beyond the Local Server: Cloud Security
questions.question: What is Software as a Service (SaaS)?

choices.text: Raw virtual servers, storage, and networks that you configure yourself.
An environment ready for code where you deploy your application and database.
A cloud deployment used only by one organization.
A SQL command that gives a privilege to a user.
A finished, complete application that you log in and use, such as Gmail or Salesforce.

questions.explanation: SaaS provides a complete application for users to consume directly.

questions.section: Moving Beyond the Local Server: Cloud Security
questions.question: Which service model gives the most control but also requires the most effort?

choices.text: IaaS.
PaaS.
SaaS.
SECaaS.
Community Cloud.

questions.explanation: The IaaS description explicitly says it requires the most effort but gives the most control.

questions.section: Moving Beyond the Local Server: Cloud Security
questions.question: What is a public cloud?

choices.text: Infrastructure dedicated exclusively to one single organization.
Provider-owned infrastructure shared over the public internet with anyone who wants to rent it.
A combination of private sensitive systems and public website systems.
Infrastructure shared only by organizations with common concerns.
A finished application such as Gmail or Salesforce.

questions.explanation: Public cloud is provider-owned shared infrastructure available over the public internet.

questions.section: Moving Beyond the Local Server: Cloud Security
questions.question: What is a private cloud?

choices.text: Provider-owned infrastructure rented by anyone over the public internet.
A combination of public and private cloud resources.
Infrastructure dedicated exclusively to one organization; it is more secure but more expensive.
Infrastructure shared by organizations with common concerns only.
A ready platform where only application code is deployed.

questions.explanation: Private cloud is dedicated to one organization and trades cost for security.

questions.section: Moving Beyond the Local Server: Cloud Security
questions.question: What is a hybrid cloud?

choices.text: A provider-owned public service rented by anyone.
Infrastructure dedicated exclusively to one organization only.
Infrastructure shared only by hospitals with common concerns.
A combination of public and private cloud, such as private sensitive data plus a public website.
A raw hardware rental where customers install their own OS.

questions.explanation: Hybrid cloud combines public and private use cases, such as keeping sensitive data private while making a website public.

questions.section: Moving Beyond the Local Server: Cloud Security
questions.question: What is a community cloud?

choices.text: Provider-owned infrastructure available to anyone over the internet.
Infrastructure dedicated to one organization only.
A combination of a private cloud and a public website.
A finished application that users only log in to use.
Infrastructure shared by a specific group of organizations with common concerns.

questions.explanation: Community cloud is shared by organizations with common concerns.

questions.section: Moving Beyond the Local Server: Cloud Security
questions.question: Which example is given for community cloud?

choices.text: Several hospitals sharing a compliant healthcare cloud.
A company keeping sensitive data private but running its public website publicly.
Anyone renting provider infrastructure over the public internet.
A single organization paying more for dedicated infrastructure.
A user logging into Gmail or Salesforce.

questions.explanation: The source gives hospitals sharing a compliant healthcare cloud as the community cloud example.

questions.section: Moving Beyond the Local Server: Cloud Security
questions.question: What does the NIST Cloud Computing Reference Architecture provide according to the note?

choices.text: A list of SQL privileges such as Select, Insert, Delete, Update, and References.
A common framework for discussing cloud concepts, focusing on what services provide rather than how they are built.
A RAID technique for mirroring and striping hard drives.
A cloud firewall that blocks suspicious SQL queries at run time.
A physical access-control policy for data owners and departments.

questions.explanation: The note says NIST provides a common framework focused on service capabilities rather than implementation details.

questions.section: The Dangers of the Cloud (Cloud Security Risks)
questions.question: Why do new security risks appear when data moves to the cloud?

choices.text: RAID stops working whenever data is in a relational table.
Cloud computing removes every need for APIs and logins.
Data leaves your own building and runs on a cloud provider's servers.
Encryption keys cannot exist outside a local secure room.
DBMS software cannot run on rented infrastructure.

questions.explanation: The risk section starts by noting that data moves from your building to provider servers.

questions.section: The Dangers of the Cloud (Cloud Security Risks)
questions.question: What is the unknown risk profile cloud risk?

choices.text: A provider isolation failure lets one customer see another customer data.
Stolen API keys let attackers delete or steal infrastructure.
Developers misconfigure databases and expose them publicly.
You cannot inspect the physical servers yourself and must trust the provider manages security properly.
Attackers rent cheap cloud servers to host malware.

questions.explanation: Unknown risk profile comes from lack of direct visibility into provider physical infrastructure.

questions.section: The Dangers of the Cloud (Cloud Security Risks)
questions.question: What is the shared technology issue in public clouds?

choices.text: API keys are stolen because user logins are weak.
A dishonest provider employee abuses administrative access.
Attackers rent cheap servers to launch attacks.
Developers accidentally leave databases open to the public internet.
Customer data may sit on the same physical hardware as other companies, so isolation failure could expose another customer's data.

questions.explanation: The source highlights shared physical hardware and reliance on isolation software.

questions.section: The Dangers of the Cloud (Cloud Security Risks)
questions.question: Why are insecure interfaces and APIs dangerous in the cloud?

choices.text: Cloud resources are controlled over the internet using APIs, so stolen API keys or weak logins can let attackers delete or steal infrastructure.
They force users to install their own operating system in SaaS applications.
They make RAID mirroring copy data to only one disk.
They prevent applications from using parameterized queries.
They make views reveal only authorized columns.

questions.explanation: API-key theft or weak login security can compromise entire infrastructure.

questions.section: The Dangers of the Cloud (Cloud Security Risks)
questions.question: What is account hijacking in the cloud risk list?

choices.text: Developers accidentally make databases public on the internet.
Attackers guess passwords or use phishing to access cloud administrator accounts.
A cloud provider employee abuses administrative access.
One tenant sees another tenant data because isolation fails.
Cloud servers are rented to host malware or launch attacks.

questions.explanation: Account hijacking targets administrator accounts through password guessing or phishing.

questions.section: The Dangers of the Cloud (Cloud Security Risks)
questions.question: What causes data loss or leakage in the cloud example?

choices.text: Cloud customers cannot inspect physical servers directly.
Provider employees always need to steal both database and key.
Developers accidentally misconfigure databases, leaving them open to the public internet.
Attackers force a DBMS to send data to another server.
Tenants use unique identifiers to separate records correctly.

questions.explanation: Cloud ease-of-change can lead to accidental public database exposure.

questions.section: The Dangers of the Cloud (Cloud Security Risks)
questions.question: Who is the malicious insider in the cloud risk list?

choices.text: A developer who creates a server from a dashboard without speaking to a human.
A customer who pays only for consumed resources.
An administrator who assigns Alice and Bob to Support Agent.
A dishonest employee at the cloud provider who abuses administrative access to steal customer data.
A DBMS that locks records to maintain integrity.

questions.explanation: The malicious insider risk involves provider employees with administrative access.

questions.section: The Dangers of the Cloud (Cloud Security Risks)
questions.question: What is abuse of cloud services?

choices.text: Customers use cloud servers to scale a viral website legitimately.
Cloud providers pool resources across customers invisibly.
Organizations route traffic through cloud security providers.
A single organization uses dedicated private cloud infrastructure.
Attackers rent cheap, powerful cloud servers to host malware or launch massive attacks.

questions.explanation: The risk is attackers using the same cheap powerful resources for malicious activity.

questions.section: Protecting Data in the Cloud
questions.question: Because customers do not own the physical hardware, what does cloud data security become?

choices.text: A shared responsibility between the customer and the cloud provider.
The exclusive responsibility of the customer with no provider role.
The exclusive responsibility of the provider with no customer role.
A RAID-only problem solved by mirroring disks.
A SQL-only problem solved by granting all privileges.

questions.explanation: Cloud security becomes a shared responsibility.

questions.section: Protecting Data in the Cloud
questions.question: What are the two main database service architecture models listed?

choices.text: Public and community only.
Multi-instance and multi-tenant.
IaaS and SaaS only.
Grant and Revoke.
Select and Update.

questions.explanation: The cloud data protection section compares multi-instance and multi-tenant models.

questions.section: Protecting Data in the Cloud
questions.question: What is the multi-instance model?

choices.text: Multiple customers share the exact same underlying database system.
A provider delivers security applications over the internet as SaaS.
A customer rents raw hardware and installs their own operating system.
Each customer gets a dedicated, isolated database system running on its own virtual machine.
A company combines private sensitive data with a public website.

questions.explanation: Multi-instance gives each customer a separate database system and VM.

questions.section: Protecting Data in the Cloud
questions.question: Which advantage belongs to the multi-instance model?

choices.text: Maximum efficiency and low cost because all customers share one database system.
Very easy provider management because tenants are separated only by tags.
No need for any virtual machine or dedicated environment.
It relies entirely on provider logic to separate all customer rows.
Strong security isolation and deep customization because the environment belongs entirely to the customer.

questions.explanation: The advantages listed are strong isolation, customization, and low risk of seeing another customer data.

questions.section: Protecting Data in the Cloud
questions.question: What is the disadvantage of the multi-instance model?

choices.text: It requires more resources, making it more expensive.
It is impossible to customize because all customers share one environment.
It has severe breach risk whenever tag logic fails.
It cannot provide strong security isolation.
It requires every customer to use Gmail or Salesforce.

questions.explanation: The source says multi-instance uses more resources and costs more.

questions.section: Protecting Data in the Cloud
questions.question: What is the multi-tenant model?

choices.text: Each customer gets a dedicated database system on its own virtual machine.
Multiple customers share the same underlying database system, with software separating their data.
Each organization buys physical servers and runs them in a secure room.
A developer creates a server without talking to a provider employee.
A user logs into a finished complete application.

questions.explanation: Multi-tenant database services share one system across tenants.

questions.section: Protecting Data in the Cloud
questions.question: How does multi-tenant software separate customer data?

choices.text: Using separate virtual machines for every customer.
Using only physical guards and locked server rooms.
Using unique identifiers or tags behind the scenes.
Using SQL Injection to infer which data belongs to whom.
Using lost encryption keys to make data unreadable.

questions.explanation: The software separates tenant data with unique identifiers or tags.

questions.section: Protecting Data in the Cloud
questions.question: Which advantages belong to the multi-tenant model?

choices.text: It provides dedicated VMs and deep customization for each customer.
It removes every risk of a breach caused by separation logic.
It costs more because each tenant has a private database system.
It is efficient, cost-effective, and easy for the provider to manage and scale.
It requires customers to install their own operating systems.

questions.explanation: Multi-tenant is efficient and cheaper because resources are shared, and providers can manage and scale it easily.

questions.section: Protecting Data in the Cloud
questions.question: What is the main security disadvantage of multi-tenant databases?

choices.text: Every tenant gets too much customization and dedicated isolation.
Every customer must manage their own physical hardware.
The model cannot share resources, so it is never cost-effective.
The model requires users to lose their encryption keys.
Security depends on provider software keeping tenant data separate; a logic flaw could cause a severe breach.

questions.explanation: The source warns that security relies on the provider's software successfully keeping the data separate; a flaw could cause a severe data breach.

questions.section: Protecting Data in the Cloud
questions.question: What does SECaaS stand for?

choices.text: Security as a Service.
Software Encryption Control as a Service.
Secure Elastic Cloud as a Service.
Server Endpoint Compliance as a Service.
Shared Encryption Cluster as a Service.

questions.explanation: SECaaS is expanded as Security as a Service.

questions.section: Protecting Data in the Cloud
questions.question: What is Security as a Service (SECaaS)?

choices.text: A provider gives every customer a dedicated database VM.
A provider delivers security applications over the internet as a SaaS.
A database copies data across drives for hardware availability.
An administrator gives privileges using SQL Grant.
An attacker sends stolen data to an out-of-band server.

questions.explanation: SECaaS delivers security tools over the internet using a SaaS model.

questions.section: Protecting Data in the Cloud
questions.question: How is SECaaS described as replacing traditional local security tools?

choices.text: Instead of using parameterized queries, developers trust every input field.
Instead of using RAID, every disk is allowed to fail independently.
Instead of buying hardware firewalls or installing heavy antivirus locally, traffic is routed through a cloud security provider.
Instead of using roles, every user is granted all table privileges.
Instead of using private cloud, every sensitive database is placed in public cloud.

questions.explanation: The SECaaS section contrasts cloud-delivered security with local hardware firewalls and heavy antivirus installations.

questions.section: Protecting Data in the Cloud
questions.question: What can SECaaS protect?

choices.text: Only the provider physical servers, never customer networks.
Only browser cookies, never cloud resources.
Only local hard drives, never cloud applications.
Both resources inside the cloud and the company's local office networks.
Only relational views, never network traffic.

questions.explanation: SECaaS can protect cloud resources and local office networks.

questions.section: Protecting Data in the Cloud
questions.question: What does Identity and Access Management (IAM) provide in the SECaaS examples?

choices.text: Filtering malicious web traffic before it reaches the application.
Continuously scanning files and activity for known attacks.
Monitoring outgoing traffic for accidental sensitive uploads.
Copying data to a safe cloud location after ransomware or destruction.
Cloud systems that manage user logins and permissions across multiple applications.

questions.explanation: IAM is listed as managing logins and permissions across applications.

questions.section: Protecting Data in the Cloud
questions.question: What do cloud firewalls do in the SECaaS examples?

choices.text: Filter malicious web traffic before it reaches the application.
Manage user logins and permissions across applications.
Scan files and activity for known attacks.
Monitor outgoing traffic for sensitive uploads.
Copy data to a safe cloud location for disaster recovery.

questions.explanation: Cloud firewalls are listed as filtering malicious web traffic before application delivery.

questions.section: Protecting Data in the Cloud
questions.question: What do threat detection and antivirus services do?

choices.text: Manage user logins and permissions across multiple applications.
Continuously scan files and activity for known attacks.
Filter malicious web traffic before it reaches the application.
Monitor outgoing traffic to stop accidental sensitive uploads.
Automatically copy data to a safe cloud location after ransomware.

questions.explanation: Threat detection and antivirus continuously scan for known attacks.

questions.section: Protecting Data in the Cloud
questions.question: What does Data Loss Prevention monitor?

choices.text: User logins and permissions across multiple applications.
Malicious web traffic before it reaches the application.
Outgoing traffic to ensure employees do not accidentally upload sensitive information.
Files and activity for known attacks only.
Automatic backup copies after ransomware.

questions.explanation: DLP is described as monitoring outgoing traffic for accidental sensitive uploads.

questions.section: Protecting Data in the Cloud
questions.question: What does the Backup and Disaster Recovery SECaaS example do?

choices.text: Manages user logins and permissions across multiple applications.
Filters malicious web traffic before it reaches the app.
Scans files and activity for known attacks only.
Automatically copies data to a safe cloud location if the main system is destroyed or hit by ransomware.
Monitors outgoing traffic for accidental sensitive uploads only.

questions.explanation: The backup and DR example copies data safely in case of destruction or ransomware.

questions.section: Protecting Data in the Cloud
questions.question: What final benefit is attributed to SECaaS?

choices.text: Organizations can stop using databases because security tools store all data.
Organizations can make every tenant share all customer data openly.
Organizations can avoid all key management by deleting encryption keys.
Organizations can guarantee no developer will ever misconfigure a database.
Organizations can use the massive computing power of the cloud to defend databases and applications more effectively than on their own.

questions.explanation: The final paragraph says SECaaS leverages cloud computing power for stronger defense than most organizations can build alone.
